request_debug.prompt_preview:
You write per-candidate post-mortems for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and one dead-startup canonical document, produce a single LLMSynthesis JSON object that explains why this dead startup is similar, where the new pitch could diverge, what killed the dead startup, and what the new founder should learn.

Rules:
- Ground every claim in the candidate document or in tool calls. Do not invent facts.
- A `Trusted facts` block (typed pipeline data — not user input) appears below the pitc

response.text: {"candidate_id":"quibi::media_content","name":"quibi","one_liner":"Short-form mobile streaming platform that raised $1.75B, launched in April 2020, and shut down within eight months after failing to convert free-trial users into paying subscribers.","similarity":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Quibi used a subscription-recurring model (with an ad-supported tier) targeting consumers on mobile. The new pitch is a free-to-play B2C game monetized through cosmetics and a battle pass — a transaction-fee/tiered-freemium model. Both are B2C and mobile-first, but the core revenue mechanic (subscription vs. in-game purchases) is fundamentally different."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Both products target consumers on mobile devices, but Quibi was a short-form video streaming service competing with YouTube and TikTok, while the pitch is in the 4X strategy gaming sector on iOS, Android, and Steam. The audiences, competitive sets, and content economics are largely distinct."},"gtm":{"score":2,"rationale":"Quibi relied on Hollywood celebrity IP, massive pre-launch ad spend, and telco partnerships to acquire subscribers. The new pitch is a gaming studio likely relying on app store discovery, community-driven content, and live-service engagement loops — very different go-to-market channels and mechanics."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Both are consumer-facing digital products targeting a broad global audience and launching on mobile platforms (iOS/Android). Both faced the challenge of converting free/trial users into paying customers. The scale ambition is comparable, though Quibi had $1.75B in pre-launch capital, which is unlikely for an indie gaming studio."}},"why_similar":"Both are B2C mobile-first consumer products that must convert a free or trial user base into a monetized one. Both face the cold-start problem of building an audience from scratch on crowded app stores (iOS and Android). Both rely on content quality and engagement to retain users. The core challenge — getting consumers to pay for entertainment on their phones when free alternatives exist — is structurally shared.","where_diverged":"Several concrete dimensions separate the new pitch from Quibi: (1) Monetization model — the pitch uses free-to-play cosmetics and a battle pass (transactional/tiered-freemium) rather than Quibi's subscription-recurring paywall, lowering the barrier to entry; (2) Platform — the pitch spans Steam (PC/desktop) in addition to mobile, broadening the addressable audience beyond mobile-only; (3) Sector — 4X strategy gaming vs. short-form video streaming; (4) Capital structure — Quibi burned $1.75B pre-launch on content licensing, while a gaming studio's variable cost structure around a battle pass is far leaner; (5) Content ownership — the new studio owns its own IP outright, whereas Quibi did not retain rights to its programming, hampering its asset value at shutdown.","failure_causes":["product-market fit mismatch (mobile-only short-form video vs. entrenched free alternatives)","subscription paywall friction with low trial-to-paid conversion (~8%)","pandemic timing disrupted the on-the-go mobile viewing use case","overspend on content ($1B+) without validating consumer demand","mobile-only restriction alienated users who wanted TV/desktop access","misalignment between celebrity-driven content strategy and what younger audiences actually wanted","inability to retain content rights, reducing acqui-hire or asset-sale value"],"lessons_for_input":["Validate the core retention loop (session length, return rate) before scaling paid acquisition — Quibi discovered demand problems only after spending $1.75B.","Free-to-play lowers the paywall friction Quibi suffered, but ensure cosmetics and the battle pass are desirable enough to drive conversion; test willingness-to-pay early with a soft launch.","Own your IP and in-game assets completely — Quibi's failure to retain content rights destroyed residual value at shutdown; a studio that owns its 4X universe can license, port, or sequel it.","Do not be mobile-only if your genre (4X strategy) has a natural home on PC/Steam; Quibi's refusal to support TV was a self-inflicted wound corrected too late.","Build community and social sharing into the product from day one — Quibi launched without social features and missed organic viral distribution that is table stakes in gaming."]}
